_metadata.description: Medication-nutrient effects — true depletions, functional antagonism, monitoring notes, condition-related effects, and supplement interactions
data_sources: Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook (Pelton et al.)
_metadata.clinical_disclaimer: Informational only. Consult healthcare provider before supplementing.
_metadata.notes: Two entries (DEP_BETABLOCKERS_MELATONIN, DEP_BENZODIAZEPINES_MELATONIN) reference canonical_id 'melatonin' which is not in rda_optimal_uls.json. These are valid clinical depletions; rda_optimal_uls.json should be extended to include melatonin if depletion checker requires canonical_id resolution.

completed_migrations.summary: Authored layperson copy fields (alert_headline, alert_body, acknowledgement_note, monitoring_tip_short) + optional adequacy_threshold + flutter_top_level_key invariant
Added optional food_sources_short field + clinical nocebo-safe validator rules (numeric stat ban, absolute claims ban, acute framing ban, catastrophizing warn, symptom-list cap, sentence cap)
Added required depletion_type taxonomy so true depletions are separated from functional antagonism, monitoring/stability notes, condition-related nutrient issues, and supplement interactions.
High-value expansion batch: isoniazid→B6, methotrexate→folate, and orlistat→fat-soluble vitamins A/D/E/K after taxonomy cleanup.
High-value expansion batch 2: sulfasalazine→folate, colchicine→B12, and cholestyramine→fat-soluble vitamins A/D/E/K.

v52_added_fields.alert_headline: authored string per entry, 20-60 chars, calm voicing (no "Depleted by X")
v52_added_fields.alert_body: authored string, 60-200 chars, includes onset framing (over time, long-term, gradually)
v52_added_fields.acknowledgement_note: authored string, 40-120 chars, positive validation shown when user's stack already covers the depletion — MUST NOT contain caution verbs
v52_added_fields.monitoring_tip_short: authored string, 40-120 chars, layperson action tip — always shown
v52_added_fields.adequacy_threshold_mcg / adequacy_thresh: optional numeric threshold; dose at or above this qualifies as adequate coverage (vs partial)
v52_added_fields.food_sources_short: optional string, 40-160 chars, inclusive framing only. For absorption-blocked depletions (metformin/B12, PPI/B12, statin/CoQ10) use the explicit hint pattern: "Because <drug> reduces <nutrient> absorption, food sources may not be enough on their own — a supplement is often more reliable."

v53_added_fields.depletion_type: Required taxonomy enum: depletion, functional_antagonism, monitoring_stability, condition_related, supplement_interaction. Flutter uses this to avoid treating interaction/monitoring rows as supplement-covered depletions.

depletions.mechanism: Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, the same early step of the mevalonate pathway the body uses to make ubiquinone (CoQ10). Placebo-controlled trials, pooled in a meta-analysis, show statin therapy lowers CoQ10 concentrations measured in blood.
depletions.clinical_impact: Whether that lower blood level causes the muscle symptoms some people report on statins is not established, and it does not by itself indicate a tissue deficiency. Trials of CoQ10 supplements for statin-related muscle symptoms disagree: some pooled analyses report improvement, others find no benefit over placebo.
depletions.recommendation: Statins can lower circulating CoQ10 levels. It is uncertain whether this contributes to muscle symptoms or whether CoQ10 supplements consistently help. Discuss persistent muscle symptoms with your prescriber, and do not stop a statin on your own.
depletions.monitoring_note: Routine blood CoQ10 testing is not part of standard care; persistent muscle symptoms are assessed clinically.

sources.label: Banach M et al. Statin therapy and plasma coenzyme Q10 concentrations — a systematic review and meta-analysis of placebo-controlled trials. Pharmacol Res. 2015;99:329-36
Ghirlanda G et al. Evidence of plasma CoQ10-lowering effect by HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors: a double-blind, placebo-controlled study. J Clin Pharmacol. 1993;33(3):226-9
Qu H et al. Effects of coenzyme Q10 on statin-induced myopathy: an updated meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. J Am Heart Assoc. 2018;7(19):e009835 — reports symptom improvement
Kennedy C et al. Effect of coenzyme Q10 on statin-associated myalgia and adherence to statin therapy: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Atherosclerosis. 2020;299:1-8 — finds no benefit

depletions.alert_headline: May lower circulating CoQ10 levels
depletions.alert_body: With regular use, statins can lower the amount of CoQ10 measured in the blood. Whether that causes symptoms is not established.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: You're taking CoQ10. Evidence that it improves statin-related muscle symptoms is mixed.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider mentioning any persistent muscle symptoms at your next visit.
depletions.food_sources_short: Dietary CoQ10 is minimal — organ meats and fatty fish contain small amounts — and the body also makes its own.
depletions.citation_review_note: Circulating-CoQ10 reduction kept (Banach 2015 meta-analysis, Ghirlanda 1993). Removed causal myopathy claim, unsupported cardiac tissue language, routine 100-200 mg recommendation, non-standard plasma CoQ10 testing, and the 100 mg comparison amount. Supplementation evidence conflicts (Qu 2018 vs Kennedy 2020) — both cited; severity lowered to mild.

depletions.mechanism: Statins may impair hepatic hydroxylation of vitamin D by competing for CYP450 enzymes involved in vitamin D metabolism. Cholesterol is also a substrate for vitamin D synthesis, so its reduction may limit precursor availability.
depletions.clinical_impact: Suboptimal vitamin D is associated with musculoskeletal pain, immune dysregulation, and increased fracture risk — symptoms that can overlap with or worsen statin side effects.
depletions.recommendation: Have your vitamin D (25-OH-D) level checked annually. Supplement with vitamin D3 if levels are below optimal range, typically 1,000–2,000 IU daily unless directed otherwise.
depletions.monitoring_note: Test serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D annually, especially in winter months or low-sunlight climates.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Vitamin D Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

depletions.alert_headline: May affect vitamin D over time
depletions.alert_body: Long-term statin use may gradually influence how the body processes vitamin D. Levels can drift lower over months, especially through winter.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — vitamin D intake helps support levels while on statin therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking about a vitamin D check at your annual visit, especially in winter.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin D include fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk and plant milks, and fortified cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Gastric acid and pepsin are required to cleave vitamin B12 from food proteins. PPIs and H2 blockers suppress acid secretion, impairing this liberation step and reducing B12 absorption from dietary sources (though crystalline B12 supplements are unaffected).
depletions.clinical_impact: B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia, peripheral neuropathy, cognitive decline, and elevated homocysteine. Risk increases significantly with long-term (>2 years) PPI use.
depletions.recommendation: If taking acid reducers long-term, consider a sublingual or methylcobalamin B12 supplement (1,000 mcg/day) which bypasses the acid-dependent absorption step. Ask your doctor about periodic B12 testing.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum B12 levels every 1–2 years with long-term PPI use. Methylmalonic acid is a more sensitive functional marker.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Vitamin B12 Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
Lam JR et al. Proton pump inhibitor and histamine 2 receptor antagonist use and vitamin B12 deficiency. JAMA. 2013

depletions.alert_headline: Can affect vitamin B12 absorption long-term
depletions.alert_body: By lowering stomach acid, these medications can gradually reduce how well vitamin B12 is absorbed from food. Over years of use, some people develop lower levels.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: You're taking B12 — a helpful way to support levels while using acid-reducing medication.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking about a B12 check if you've been using these medications long-term.
depletions.food_sources_short: Because these medications can limit B12 absorption, food sources may not be enough on their own — a supplement is often more reliable.

depletions.mechanism: Long-term PPI use impairs active magnesium transport in the intestine, likely by interfering with TRPM6/TRPM7 channels responsible for intestinal magnesium absorption. The exact mechanism is not fully elucidated.
depletions.clinical_impact: Hypomagnesemia from PPIs can cause muscle cramps, tremors, cardiac arrhythmias, and — by impairing PTH secretion — secondary hypocalcemia and hypokalemia that are refractory to replacement until magnesium is corrected.
depletions.recommendation: If you have been on a PPI for more than one year, ask your doctor to check your magnesium level. A magnesium glycinate or citrate supplement (200–400 mg/day) may be appropriate.
depletions.monitoring_note: FDA issued a Drug Safety Communication in 2011 requiring magnesium monitoring for patients on long-term PPIs. Check serum magnesium at baseline and periodically.

sources.label: Hess MW et al. Systematic review: hypomagnesaemia induced by proton pump inhibition. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2012
FDA Drug Safety Communication: Low magnesium levels can be associated with long-term use of PPIs. 2011

depletions.alert_headline: May lower magnesium with long-term use
depletions.alert_body: Over time, long-term PPI use can gradually reduce magnesium levels. This develops slowly over months to years and may be subtle at first.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — your magnesium intake supports maintaining levels while using this medication.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider checking magnesium if you've been on this medication long-term.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of magnesium include leafy greens, nuts, seeds, whole grains, beans, and dark chocolate.

depletions.mechanism: Stomach acid converts insoluble calcium carbonate into absorbable ionized calcium. PPIs strongly suppress acid, so calcium carbonate taken on an empty stomach is absorbed less well. Calcium citrate does not need acid, and taking carbonate with food restores absorption.
depletions.clinical_impact: Long-term PPI use is linked in observational studies to a modestly higher fracture risk (hip, wrist, spine), behind a 2010 FDA safety communication. The association is modest and partly confounded, and is not seen with H2 blockers.
depletions.recommendation: If you take a PPI long-term, prefer calcium citrate (it does not need stomach acid to absorb) or take calcium carbonate with a meal. Aim for adequate calcium and vitamin D, and ask your doctor about bone health.
depletions.monitoring_note: Consider DEXA scan for bone density assessment with long-term PPI use, especially postmenopausal women.

sources.label: Recker RR. Calcium absorption and achlorhydria. N Engl J Med. 1985
O'Connell MB et al. Effects of proton pump inhibitors on calcium carbonate absorption in women: a randomized crossover trial. Am J Med. 2005
Yang YX et al. Long-term proton pump inhibitor therapy and risk of hip fracture. JAMA. 2006
Poly TN et al. Proton pump inhibitors and risk of hip fracture: a meta-analysis of observational studies. Osteoporos Int. 2019

Serfaty-Lacrosniere C et al. Hypochlorhydria from short-term omeprazole treatment does not inhibit intestinal absorption of calcium, phosphorus, magnesium or zinc from food in humans. J Am Coll Nutr. 1995

depletions.alert_headline: May affect calcium absorption over years
depletions.alert_body: Long-term PPI use can gradually reduce how well calcium carbonate is absorbed, which over years may influence bone strength. H2 blockers and occasional antacids are not a concern here.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — calcium intake is part of standard support during long-term acid-reducer use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing bone health and calcium form with your doctor; citrate is often preferred.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of calcium include dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens, almonds, and canned sardines or salmon with bones.

depletions.mechanism: Gastric acid reduces dietary non-heme iron (Fe3+) to the absorbable ferrous form (Fe2+) and frees iron from food. Both PPIs and H2 blockers suppress acid, so long-term use can lower non-heme iron absorption; the effect is dose-related and reverses after stopping.
depletions.clinical_impact: Over months to years this can contribute to iron deficiency and, if unaddressed, iron-deficiency anemia — most relevant for menstruating women, frequent blood donors, and people with low dietary iron. Heme iron from meat is less affected.
depletions.recommendation: If you take a PPI or H2 blocker long-term and are at risk (menstruating, low iron, plant-based diet), ask your doctor to check ferritin. Take any iron supplement a couple of hours apart from your acid reducer, and with vitamin C to aid absorption.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum ferritin and CBC annually with long-term PPI use, especially in women of childbearing age.

sources.label: Lam JR et al. Proton pump inhibitor and histamine-2 receptor antagonist use and iron deficiency. Gastroenterology. 2017
Hutchinson C et al. Proton pump inhibitors suppress absorption of dietary non-haem iron in hereditary haemochromatosis. Gut. 2007

depletions.alert_headline: May lower iron absorption over time
depletions.alert_body: Long-term use of acid-reducing medications can gradually reduce iron absorption from food. Some people develop lower iron stores over months of use.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: You're taking iron — a thoughtful step while using acid-reducing medication long-term.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking about periodic ferritin checks, particularly for women of childbearing age.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of iron include red meat, poultry, seafood, beans, lentils, tofu, spinach, and fortified cereals.

depletions.mechanism: The acidic gastric environment normally stabilizes vitamin C (ascorbic acid). Elevated gastric pH from acid suppression accelerates oxidation of ascorbic acid to dehydroascorbic acid, reducing its bioavailability. Gastric secretion of vitamin C is also reduced.
depletions.clinical_impact: Mild vitamin C depletion may impair immune function, collagen synthesis, and antioxidant defenses. The clinical significance is generally modest in individuals with adequate dietary intake.
depletions.recommendation: Ensure adequate vitamin C intake from fruits and vegetables. If supplementing, take vitamin C away from antacid doses to minimize pH-related degradation.

sources.label: Henry EB et al. Proton pump inhibitors reduce the bioavailability of dietary vitamin C. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2005
Mowat C et al. Omeprazole and dietary nitrate independently affect levels of vitamin C and nitrite in gastric juice. Gastroenterology. 1999

depletions.alert_headline: May modestly affect vitamin C over time
depletions.alert_body: Higher stomach pH from acid-reducers can gradually reduce how well vitamin C holds up through digestion. The effect is typically modest with regular use.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — your vitamin C intake supports levels while using acid-reducing medication.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider taking vitamin C at a different time of day from acid-reducing medication.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin C include citrus fruits, strawberries, bell peppers, broccoli, kiwi, and tomatoes.
depletions.citation_review_note: Rejected as a consumer depletion warning (evidence-based, not a data gap). Only one small systemic study (Henry 2005: omeprazole 40 mg x 4 wk → plasma vitamin C -12.3%, authors state 'clinical significance is unclear', concentrated in H. pylori+/low-baseline). The remaining literature is intragastric gastric-juice ascorbate (an N-nitrosation/cancer mechanism, not body vitamin C status). No clinically meaningful depletion; no H2/antacid data. Retired the Pelton handbook citation.

depletions.mechanism: Zinc absorption from food is partially dependent on gastric acid solubilization. Elevated gastric pH impairs the release of zinc from food-bound complexes, reducing bioavailability of dietary zinc.
depletions.clinical_impact: Zinc deficiency affects immune function, wound healing, taste and smell, and reproductive health. Long-term marginal zinc depletion is most concerning in elderly patients with already-borderline intake.
depletions.recommendation: Ensure dietary zinc adequacy. If on long-term antacids, consider a modest zinc supplement (10–15 mg/day as zinc glycinate). Avoid taking zinc simultaneously with the acid-reducing medication.

sources.label: Sturniolo GC et al. Inhibition of gastric acid secretion reduces zinc absorption in man. J Am Coll Nutr. 1991
Ozutemiz AO et al. Effect of omeprazole on plasma zinc levels after oral zinc administration. Indian J Gastroenterol. 2002 (PMID 12546170)

Serfaty-Lacrosniere C et al. Hypochlorhydria from short-term omeprazole treatment does not inhibit intestinal absorption of calcium, phosphorus, magnesium or zinc from food in humans. J Am Coll Nutr. 1995

depletions.alert_headline: May slightly lower zinc over time
depletions.alert_body: With long-term use, acid-reducing medications can gradually reduce how well zinc is freed from food and absorbed. The effect is usually mild.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — your zinc intake helps support levels while on acid-reducing medication.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider taking zinc at a different time of day from acid-reducing medication.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of zinc include oysters, meat, poultry, beans, lentils, nuts, seeds, and whole grains.
depletions.citation_review_note: Rejected as a consumer depletion warning (evidence-based, not a data gap). Support exists only for reduced acute absorption of a fasting soluble zinc-salt load (H2: Sturniolo 1991; PPI: Ozutemiz 2002) — a supplement-timing pharmacokinetic effect. A food-based study (Serfaty-Lacrosniere 1995) found NO change in zinc absorption from a meal, and no study shows actual zinc deficiency from acid suppression. Retired the Pelton handbook citation.

depletions.mechanism: Metformin impairs B12 absorption by interfering with a calcium-dependent intrinsic factor-B12 receptor complex on the ileal mucosa. This disrupts the final step of B12 uptake from the small intestine. The effect is dose-dependent and reverses with calcium supplementation in some patients.
depletions.clinical_impact: Roughly 6-30% of long-term metformin users show low or deficient B12, depending on how deficiency is defined and how long metformin is taken. Low B12 can cause peripheral neuropathy (which may be misattributed to diabetic neuropathy), megaloblastic anemia, and cognitive changes, and may develop insidiously over years.
depletions.recommendation: The ADA recommends periodic B12 monitoring for all patients on metformin. Supplement with sublingual methylcobalamin 1,000 mcg/day if levels are borderline or low.
depletions.monitoring_note: ADA Standards of Care recommend checking B12 levels every 2–3 years in all metformin-treated patients. Methylmalonic acid is a sensitive functional marker for early deficiency.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Vitamin B12 Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
de Jager J et al. Long term treatment with metformin in patients with type 2 diabetes and risk of vitamin B-12 deficiency. BMJ. 2010
Bauman WA et al. Increased intake of calcium reverses vitamin B12 malabsorption induced by metformin. Diabetes Care. 2000

depletions.alert_headline: May lower vitamin B12 over time
depletions.alert_body: With long-term use, metformin can reduce how well vitamin B12 is absorbed. Some people develop lower levels over years, which may show up as fatigue or tingling in the hands and feet.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — you're taking B12, which aligns with guidance for long-term metformin use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider checking B12 levels every 2-3 years; easy to review at a routine visit.
depletions.food_sources_short: Because metformin reduces B12 absorption, food sources may not be enough on their own — a supplement is often more reliable.

depletions.mechanism: Metformin may reduce plasma folate levels through inhibition of intestinal folate transport. Some studies also suggest AMPK activation by metformin alters folate-dependent one-carbon metabolism.
depletions.clinical_impact: Reduced folate elevates homocysteine, increasing cardiovascular risk, and may impair DNA methylation and cell division. Particularly important in women of childbearing age (neural tube defect risk).
depletions.recommendation: Ensure adequate folate intake through diet and/or a daily multivitamin with 400–800 mcg folate (or methylfolate for those with MTHFR variants).
depletions.monitoring_note: Consider checking plasma homocysteine as a functional marker of folate/B12 adequacy in long-term metformin users.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Folate Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
de Jager J et al. Long term treatment with metformin in patients with type 2 diabetes and risk of vitamin B-12 deficiency. BMJ. 2010

depletions.alert_headline: May modestly affect folate over time
depletions.alert_body: With long-term metformin use, folate levels can drift lower over months. The effect is usually modest, but folate matters more when planning for pregnancy.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — folate intake is a thoughtful complement to long-term metformin use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing folate at your routine visits, especially when planning ahead.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of folate include leafy greens, lentils, chickpeas, asparagus, avocado, and fortified grains and cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Estrogen-containing oral contraceptives are associated with lower plasma pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (the active form of vitamin B6) in population studies. The effect is modest with today's low-dose formulations and was larger with the higher-estrogen pills of earlier decades.
depletions.clinical_impact: The change in B6 status is usually subclinical. The main practical concern is entering pregnancy with reduced B6 reserves in someone who stops the pill and conceives soon after.
depletions.recommendation: A normal diet (poultry, fish, potatoes, chickpeas, bananas) or a standard multivitamin typically provides enough B6 — high-dose B6 supplements are not recommended, because chronic intake well above the daily requirement can cause nerve symptoms. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss B6 and folate with your clinician.

sources.label: Wilson SMC et al. Oral contraceptive use: impact on folate, vitamin B6, and vitamin B12 status. Nutr Rev. 2011;69(10):572-83
NIH ODS — Vitamin B6 Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

depletions.alert_headline: May lower vitamin B6 over time
depletions.alert_body: Combined hormonal birth control is linked to modestly lower vitamin B6 (PLP) levels in some people over months of use. Levels usually stay in a range that isn't clinically obvious.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — B6 intake supports levels while using hormonal birth control.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: If you are planning pregnancy, consider discussing B6 and folate adequacy with your clinician.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin B6 include poultry, fish, potatoes, chickpeas, bananas, and fortified cereals.
depletions.citation_review_note: Downgraded to possible evidence (Wilson 2011); 25–50 mg B6 recommendation cut — chronic high-dose B6 exceeds the EFSA 12 mg UL (neuropathy risk); reframed around pre-pregnancy adequacy.

depletions.mechanism: Oral contraceptives appear to reduce serum B12 by lowering plasma binding proteins (transcobalamin I and II), redistributing B12 into tissues and reducing measured serum levels. Intestinal absorption is not significantly impaired.
depletions.clinical_impact: Low serum B12, even if partially artifactual from protein binding changes, may contribute to fatigue, mood disturbances, and elevated homocysteine. Women planning pregnancy after stopping OCPs should ensure B12 repletion.
depletions.recommendation: Include B12-rich foods (meat, fish, dairy, eggs) in your diet, or take a B-complex supplement. Particularly important when transitioning off OCPs and planning pregnancy.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Vitamin B12 Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

depletions.alert_headline: May modestly affect vitamin B12 over time
depletions.alert_body: With regular use, hormonal birth control can shift how vitamin B12 appears in the blood over months, sometimes showing slightly lower levels.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — B12 intake helps support levels while using hormonal birth control.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing B12 at a routine visit, especially if planning pregnancy ahead.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin B12 include fish, meat, poultry, eggs, dairy, and fortified plant milks and cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Estrogen components of OCPs impair folate metabolism and reduce serum folate levels. The mechanism involves altered folate binding protein expression and possibly impaired intestinal folate absorption.
depletions.clinical_impact: Folate depletion increases homocysteine and neural tube defect risk. Women stopping OCPs who become pregnant within months may have insufficient folate stores during critical early fetal development.
depletions.recommendation: Take a daily multivitamin with 400–800 mcg folate (methylfolate preferred) throughout OCP use. This is especially critical for anyone who may become pregnant after stopping.
depletions.monitoring_note: Consider starting 5-methyltetrahydrofolate supplementation at least 3 months before attempting conception after stopping OCPs.

sources.label: Wilson SMC et al. Oral contraceptive use: impact on folate, vitamin B6, and vitamin B12 status. Nutr Rev. 2011;69(10):572-83 — finds current OCs do not negatively impact folate status

depletions.alert_headline: May slightly lower folate over time
depletions.alert_body: With regular use, oral contraceptives can modestly reduce folate levels over time. Most people remain within range, but folate becomes more important when planning for pregnancy.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — maintaining folate intake is a thoughtful step, especially if pregnancy may be a future consideration.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing folate intake during routine visits, particularly when planning ahead.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of folate include leafy greens, lentils, chickpeas, asparagus, avocado, and fortified grains and cereals.
depletions.citation_review_note: Rejected: modern low-dose oral contraceptives do not meaningfully deplete folate (Wilson 2011, Nutr Rev — older depletion evidence was confounded and used higher-estrogen pills). Preconception folate adequacy is standard prenatal guidance independent of OCP use.

depletions.mechanism: Estrogen and progesterone in OCPs promote cellular uptake and urinary excretion of magnesium, resulting in lower plasma magnesium levels. Hormonal effects on renal magnesium handling are also implicated.
depletions.clinical_impact: Magnesium deficiency contributes to muscle cramps, headaches/migraines, anxiety, poor sleep, and insulin resistance. Low magnesium is also associated with worsening PMS symptoms.
depletions.recommendation: Consider a daily magnesium glycinate or citrate supplement (200–400 mg). Magnesium may also help with OCP-related headaches and mood changes.

sources.label: Palmery M et al. Oral contraceptives and changes in nutritional requirements. Eur Rev Med Pharmacol Sci. 2013

depletions.alert_headline: May lower magnesium over time
depletions.alert_body: With regular use, hormonal birth control can gradually shift magnesium levels lower. Some people notice muscle tension or headaches with long-term use.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — your magnesium intake supports levels while on hormonal birth control.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing magnesium at a routine visit if you notice frequent headaches.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of magnesium include leafy greens, nuts, seeds, whole grains, beans, and dark chocolate.

depletions.mechanism: Estrogens increase plasma zinc-binding proteins, redistributing zinc from plasma to tissues and reducing measurable serum zinc. Urinary zinc excretion may also increase with estrogen use.
depletions.clinical_impact: Reduced zinc availability affects immune function, skin health, wound healing, and hormonal balance. Zinc is also important for reproductive health when planning pregnancy after stopping OCPs.
depletions.recommendation: Ensure adequate dietary zinc (meat, shellfish, legumes, seeds) or take a modest zinc supplement (10–15 mg/day as zinc glycinate). Do not exceed 40 mg/day from all sources.

sources.label: Palmery M et al. Oral contraceptives and changes in nutritional requirements. Eur Rev Med Pharmacol Sci. 2013

depletions.alert_headline: May modestly affect zinc over time
depletions.alert_body: Hormonal birth control can gradually shift how zinc is distributed in the body over months. Measured levels sometimes run lower with long-term use.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — zinc intake helps support levels while using hormonal birth control.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider a balanced multivitamin that includes zinc; discuss with your clinician.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of zinc include meat, poultry, shellfish, beans, lentils, nuts, seeds, and whole grains.

depletions.mechanism: Estrogens increase the rate of vitamin C catabolism and may compete with vitamin C for cellular transport via SVCT transporters. Plasma ascorbic acid levels are consistently lower in OCP users.
depletions.clinical_impact: Mild reduction in vitamin C status may subtly impair antioxidant defenses and collagen synthesis. The clinical significance is typically modest in women with adequate dietary intake.
depletions.recommendation: Ensure daily vitamin C intake of at least 75 mg (the RDA for women) through fruits and vegetables, or supplement with 250–500 mg daily.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May slightly lower vitamin C over time
depletions.alert_body: With regular use, hormonal birth control can gradually shift vitamin C levels lower. The effect is usually modest in people with a varied diet.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — your vitamin C intake supports levels while on hormonal birth control.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider daily fruit and vegetable variety; a supplement is optional.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin C include citrus fruits, strawberries, bell peppers, broccoli, kiwi, and tomatoes.

depletions.mechanism: OCP use is associated with lower plasma alpha-tocopherol levels, possibly due to increased oxidative stress from estrogen-driven lipid peroxidation consuming vitamin E as a fat-soluble antioxidant.
depletions.clinical_impact: Mild vitamin E reduction has limited overt clinical symptoms but may contribute to increased oxidative stress and slightly impaired immune response over time.
depletions.recommendation: A balanced diet rich in nuts, seeds, and vegetable oils generally provides sufficient vitamin E. A low-dose daily supplement (15 mg/day) may be considered for women on long-term OCPs.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May slightly lower vitamin E over time
depletions.alert_body: With regular use, hormonal birth control can modestly reduce vitamin E levels over months. Most people feel no change; a varied diet usually covers this.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin E intake supports antioxidant balance during long-term use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider variety in nuts, seeds, and vegetable oils in daily meals.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin E include almonds, sunflower seeds, vegetable oils, avocado, and leafy greens.

depletions.mechanism: Loop diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide) and thiazide diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide, chlorthalidone) increase urine volume and renal potassium wasting. They inhibit sodium reabsorption, which increases tubular flow and potassium secretion in the collecting duct.
depletions.clinical_impact: Hypokalemia causes muscle weakness, leg cramps, fatigue, constipation, and dangerously abnormal heart rhythms (arrhythmias). Particularly serious in patients also taking digoxin, where hypokalemia increases toxicity risk.
depletions.recommendation: Potassium-rich foods (bananas, potatoes, spinach) are often recommended, but many patients require potassium supplementation (typically 20–40 mEq/day as prescribed). Regular monitoring is essential.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum potassium at baseline and within 1–2 weeks of starting or dose-changing a diuretic. Ongoing monitoring frequency depends on dose and clinical stability.

sources.label: Brater DC. Diuretic therapy. N Engl J Med. 1998;339:387-395

depletions.alert_headline: Can lower potassium with regular use
depletions.alert_body: Certain diuretics increase potassium loss through urine. With ongoing use over weeks to months, levels may gradually decline.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — potassium is typically monitored closely with these medications, and your care plan helps keep levels on track.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Ask about periodic potassium checks; this is a standard part of follow-up.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of potassium include bananas, oranges, potatoes, beans, yogurt, and tomatoes.

depletions.mechanism: Loop and thiazide diuretics increase renal magnesium excretion by inhibiting magnesium reabsorption in the thick ascending limb of Henle and distal tubule. The increased tubular flow rate also washes out magnesium.
depletions.clinical_impact: Magnesium depletion makes potassium replacement difficult (refractory hypokalemia) and increases arrhythmia risk. Muscle cramps, weakness, and tremors are common symptoms.
depletions.recommendation: Supplement with magnesium glycinate or citrate (200–400 mg/day). Correcting magnesium is often required to successfully replete potassium in diuretic-treated patients.
depletions.monitoring_note: Serum magnesium should be checked alongside potassium in patients on diuretics. Note that serum magnesium can be normal despite intracellular depletion.

sources.label: Ellison DH. Divalent cation transport by the distal nephron. Am J Physiol Renal Physiol. 2000;279(4):F616-25
Dai LJ et al. Cellular mechanisms of chlorothiazide and cellular potassium depletion on Mg2+ uptake in distal convoluted tubule cells. Kidney Int. 1997;51(4):1008-17

depletions.alert_headline: Can lower magnesium with regular use
depletions.alert_body: Loop and thiazide diuretics can gradually increase magnesium loss through urine over weeks to months. Keeping magnesium steady often helps keep potassium steady too.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — your magnesium intake supports steady levels while on diuretic therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Ask about periodic magnesium checks alongside your potassium tests.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of magnesium include leafy greens, nuts, seeds, whole grains, beans, and dark chocolate.

depletions.mechanism: Thiazide diuretics increase urinary zinc excretion; loop diuretics have a much smaller effect. Over long-term use this can modestly lower tissue zinc, although blood zinc usually remains in the normal range.
depletions.clinical_impact: A clear zinc deficiency from thiazides has not been established — blood zinc typically stays normal — so this is a mild, long-term consideration. It is most relevant for people whose dietary zinc is already low.
depletions.recommendation: A true shortfall is unlikely. If you take a thiazide long-term and eat little zinc-rich food, a modest zinc intake (10-15 mg/day) or a multivitamin is reasonable. Zinc from meat, shellfish, and legumes covers most needs.

sources.label: Wester PO. Urinary zinc excretion during treatment with different diuretics. Acta Med Scand. 1980
Golik A et al. Hydrochlorothiazide-amiloride causes excessive urinary zinc excretion. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 1987
Wester PO. Tissue zinc at autopsy - relation to medication with diuretics. Acta Med Scand. 1980
Mountokalakis T et al. Zinc deficiency in mild hypertensive patients treated with diuretics. J Hypertens Suppl. 1984

depletions.alert_headline: Thiazides may lower zinc over time
depletions.alert_body: Thiazide diuretics can gradually increase urinary zinc loss with long-term use. Blood zinc usually stays normal, though tissue stores may drift a little lower over time.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good - your zinc intake supports levels while on long-term thiazide therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider a multivitamin with zinc; ask your clinician if it's a fit.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of zinc include meat, poultry, shellfish, beans, lentils, nuts, seeds, and whole grains.

depletions.mechanism: Loop diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide) inhibit the NKCC2 transporter in the thick ascending limb, which is also needed for calcium reabsorption, so more calcium is lost in the urine. Thiazide diuretics do the opposite and retain calcium.
depletions.clinical_impact: Long-term loop-diuretic use is linked to a modestly higher fracture risk, mainly in older adults, and part of that may reflect falls rather than bone loss alone. It is a modest effect and does not by itself mean a calcium shortfall.
depletions.recommendation: If you take a loop diuretic long-term, aim for adequate dietary calcium and vitamin D and ask your doctor about bone health. Most people do not need high-dose calcium supplements.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum calcium and consider bone density assessment in patients on long-term loop diuretic therapy.

sources.label: Rejnmark L et al. Fracture risk in patients treated with loop diuretics. J Intern Med. 2006
Corrao G et al. Antihypertensive medications, loop diuretics, and risk of hip fracture in the elderly. Drugs Aging. 2015
Warshaw BL et al. The effect of chronic furosemide administration on urinary calcium excretion and calcium balance in growing rats. Pediatr Res. 1980

depletions.alert_headline: Loop diuretics may lower calcium over time
depletions.alert_body: Loop diuretics like furosemide can gradually increase calcium loss in urine with long-term use. This applies to loop diuretics, not thiazides.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — calcium intake supports bone health during long-term loop diuretic therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing bone health screening if on a loop diuretic long-term.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of calcium include dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens, almonds, and canned sardines or salmon with bones.

depletions.mechanism: Furosemide increases urine flow, and thiamine is carried out with it, so urinary thiamine loss rises. The effect tracks with the degree of diuresis and is greatest with higher doses and long-term use.
depletions.clinical_impact: In people on chronic, higher-dose furosemide — especially for heart failure — this can lead to thiamine deficiency, which may worsen heart function and, when severe, affect the nervous system. Everyday low-dose users with good intake are at much lower risk.
depletions.recommendation: If you take furosemide long-term, especially for heart failure, ask your doctor about checking thiamine or taking a thiamine-containing supplement. Do not change your diuretic on your own.
depletions.monitoring_note: Consider measuring whole-blood thiamine in heart failure patients on chronic high-dose furosemide, especially those with poor nutritional status.

sources.label: Seligmann H et al. Thiamine deficiency in patients with congestive heart failure receiving long-term furosemide therapy: a pilot study. Am J Med. 1991
Zenuk C et al. Thiamine deficiency in congestive heart failure patients receiving long term furosemide therapy. Can J Clin Pharmacol. 2003
Hanninen SA et al. The prevalence of thiamin deficiency in hospitalized patients with congestive heart failure. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2006
Rieck J et al. Urinary loss of thiamine is increased by low doses of furosemide in healthy volunteers. J Lab Clin Med. 1999

depletions.alert_headline: Furosemide may lower thiamine over time
depletions.alert_body: Furosemide can gradually increase thiamine loss in urine with ongoing use over months. This matters especially for people managing heart failure.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — thiamine intake supports levels while on long-term furosemide.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking your cardiologist about a thiamine supplement with long-term furosemide.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of thiamine include pork, fish, whole grains, fortified cereals, beans, lentils, and seeds.

depletions.mechanism: Triamterene is a structural analog of folate and a weak inhibitor of dihydrofolate reductase, the enzyme that activates folate. At usual doses cells largely compensate, so the effect on folate status is modest.
depletions.clinical_impact: For most folate-replete people at normal doses, meaningful folate depletion is uncommon. The concern is greater in pregnancy (a class of folate-blocking drugs is linked to higher birth-defect rates), in people already low in folate, in heavy alcohol use, or when combined with another folate antagonist such as methotrexate.
depletions.recommendation: Most people on triamterene do not need a folate supplement. If you are pregnant, planning pregnancy, have low folate, or take methotrexate, discuss folate with your doctor.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum folate and homocysteine in patients on long-term triamterene therapy.

sources.label: Sidhom MB et al. Monitoring the effect of triamterene and hydrochlorothiazide on dihydrofolate reductase activity. J Pharm Biomed Anal. 1989
Schalhorn A et al. Antifolate effect of triamterene on human leucocytes and on a human lymphoma cell line. Eur J Clin Pharmacol. 1981
Hernandez-Diaz S et al. Folic acid antagonists during pregnancy and the risk of birth defects. N Engl J Med. 2000

depletions.alert_headline: Triamterene may lower folate over time
depletions.alert_body: Over long-term use triamterene can mildly interfere with folate, but at usual doses the effect is small for most people. It matters more in pregnancy or if folate is already low.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — folate is a sensible thing to cover, especially in pregnancy or if your folate tends to run low.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing folate with your doctor if you are pregnant, planning pregnancy, or also take methotrexate.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of folate include leafy greens, lentils, chickpeas, asparagus, avocado, and fortified grains and cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Systemic glucocorticoids reduce intestinal calcium absorption and increase urinary calcium loss, while also lowering bone formation and increasing bone resorption. The net effect is negative calcium balance and bone loss, so the concern is bone strength rather than a measurable drop in blood calcium.
depletions.clinical_impact: Prolonged systemic glucocorticoid use — more than 3 months at about 2.5 mg/day prednisone-equivalent or more — raises the risk of osteoporosis and fracture. This applies to tablets and injections taken over months, not to short courses, inhalers, creams, or a single joint injection.
depletions.recommendation: With prolonged systemic corticosteroid use, clinicians may assess calcium and vitamin D intake, fracture risk, and whether bone-protective treatment is needed. Guideline care is directed by your individual fracture risk rather than an automatic supplement dose.
depletions.monitoring_note: American College of Rheumatology guidance recommends assessing fracture risk soon after starting long-term glucocorticoids — clinical fracture assessment, bone mineral density with vertebral fracture assessment, and FRAX scoring for adults 40 and older.

sources.label: Humphrey MB et al. 2022 American College of Rheumatology Guideline for the Prevention and Treatment of Glucocorticoid-Induced Osteoporosis. Arthritis Rheumatol. 2023;75(12):2088-2102
Ferrari P. Cortisol and the renal handling of electrolytes: role in glucocorticoid-induced hypertension and bone disease. Best Pract Res Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2003;17(4):575-89

depletions.alert_headline: Can affect calcium balance with long-term systemic use
depletions.alert_body: Taken systemically over months, corticosteroids can reduce calcium absorption and increase calcium loss, which affects bone strength over time.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: You're taking calcium — sensible with long-term steroid use. How much you need depends on your diet and bone health.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing bone health and fracture-risk screening if systemic use continues.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of calcium include dairy products, fortified plant milks, leafy greens, almonds, and canned sardines or salmon with bones.
depletions.citation_review_note: Scope narrowed to prolonged systemic use (ACR 2022: >3 months at ≥2.5 mg/day); removed the universal 'all patients should take 1,000-1,500 mg calcium' recommendation and the 500 mg comparison amount (guideline targets TOTAL intake including diet, which the app cannot observe). Mechanism cited to Ferrari 2003.

depletions.mechanism: Long-term systemic glucocorticoid therapy increases bone loss and fracture risk, and vitamin D status is assessed as part of that bone-health management. There is no reliable evidence that corticosteroids themselves directly lower vitamin D levels — studies reporting low vitamin D in steroid-treated patients are confounded by the underlying illness and reduced sun exposure.
depletions.clinical_impact: Vitamin D matters here because of bone health during prolonged steroid therapy, not because the medication drains it.
depletions.recommendation: With prolonged systemic corticosteroid use, clinicians commonly assess vitamin D status and intake as part of bone-health management. Ask your clinician whether testing or supplementation is appropriate for you — there is no universal dose.
depletions.monitoring_note: American College of Rheumatology guidance recommends assessing fracture risk soon after starting long-term glucocorticoids; vitamin D status is commonly reviewed alongside it.

sources.label: Humphrey MB et al. 2022 American College of Rheumatology Guideline for the Prevention and Treatment of Glucocorticoid-Induced Osteoporosis. Arthritis Rheumatol. 2023;75(12):2088-2102

depletions.alert_headline: Vitamin D is monitored during long-term steroid use
depletions.alert_body: Long-term systemic corticosteroid use raises bone-loss risk, so clinicians often review vitamin D as part of bone care. This is a monitoring consideration, not a sign the medication drains vitamin D.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: You're taking vitamin D. Whether you need it, and how much, is worth confirming with your clinician.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking whether a vitamin D check fits your bone-health plan.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin D include fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk and plant milks, and fortified cereals.
depletions.citation_review_note: Retyped depletion → monitoring_stability: no reliable human evidence of a direct corticosteroid-driven vitamin D depletion (Peracchi 2014 found no association with medication intake; other reports are confounded cross-sectional or veterinary). The 'hepatic CYP24A1 induction' mechanism was removed as unsupported (CYP24A1 is the renal/target-tissue 24-hydroxylase). Vitamin D assessment during prolonged systemic GC therapy is guideline-backed (ACR 2022).

depletions.mechanism: Corticosteroids, particularly those with mineralocorticoid activity (prednisone, hydrocortisone), increase sodium retention and potassium excretion in the kidney through activation of aldosterone receptors in the collecting duct.
depletions.clinical_impact: Hypokalemia causes muscle weakness, fatigue, leg cramps, constipation, and cardiac arrhythmias. Risk is highest with high-dose or long-term therapy and with drugs having significant mineralocorticoid activity.
depletions.recommendation: Increase dietary potassium (bananas, sweet potatoes, leafy greens). Prescription potassium supplementation may be required for documented hypokalemia. Regular serum potassium monitoring is standard of care.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum electrolytes (potassium, sodium) at baseline and periodically during corticosteroid therapy.

sources.label: Schimmer BP, Funder JW. ACTH, Adrenal Steroids, and Pharmacology of the Adrenal Cortex. In: Goodman & Gilman's. 13th ed.

depletions.alert_headline: Can lower potassium with regular use
depletions.alert_body: Corticosteroids can gradually increase potassium loss through the kidneys with regular use over weeks to months. The effect is more noticeable at higher doses.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — potassium is typically monitored during corticosteroid therapy, and your care plan helps keep levels steady.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Ask about periodic potassium checks during long-term corticosteroid therapy.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of potassium include bananas, oranges, potatoes, beans, yogurt, tomatoes, and leafy greens.

depletions.mechanism: Corticosteroids increase renal magnesium excretion through aldosterone-like effects on tubular transport. Reduced intestinal magnesium absorption has also been documented.
depletions.clinical_impact: Magnesium depletion worsens muscle weakness, increases muscle cramp frequency, impairs bone metabolism, and can cause refractory hypokalemia by impairing potassium retention.
depletions.recommendation: Supplement with magnesium glycinate or citrate (200–400 mg/day) during prolonged corticosteroid therapy. Adequate magnesium also supports bone health alongside calcium and vitamin D.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May lower magnesium with long-term use
depletions.alert_body: Long-term corticosteroid use can gradually increase magnesium loss through urine over months. Magnesium also supports bone health alongside calcium and vitamin D.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — magnesium intake supports bone and muscle balance during long-term corticosteroid use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking about magnesium alongside calcium and vitamin D during therapy.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of magnesium include leafy greens, nuts, seeds, whole grains, beans, and dark chocolate.

depletions.mechanism: Physiological and pharmacological stress states (including corticosteroid use) markedly increase vitamin C utilization. Corticosteroids may also increase vitamin C catabolism and excretion by the adrenal glands, which require vitamin C for cortisol synthesis.
depletions.clinical_impact: Vitamin C depletion impairs collagen synthesis (worsening skin thinning — already a side effect of steroids), reduces immune defense, and increases oxidative damage. Wound healing is compromised.
depletions.recommendation: Supplement with 250–1,000 mg vitamin C daily during corticosteroid therapy, particularly if on high doses or long-term therapy.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May lower vitamin C over time
depletions.alert_body: With long-term corticosteroid use, the body's vitamin C turnover gradually increases. Some people develop lower levels over months of therapy.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin C intake supports skin and tissue health during long-term corticosteroid use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider daily fruits and vegetables; a supplement is reasonable during therapy.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin C include citrus fruits, strawberries, bell peppers, broccoli, kiwi, and tomatoes.

depletions.mechanism: Corticosteroids increase urinary zinc excretion through mechanisms that include altered zinc transporter expression and reduced tubular reabsorption. They also impair metallothionein-mediated zinc storage.
depletions.clinical_impact: Zinc depletion impairs wound healing, immune function, and skin integrity. Particularly relevant given that skin thinning and impaired wound healing are already recognized corticosteroid side effects.
depletions.recommendation: Consider a zinc supplement (10–15 mg/day) with long-term corticosteroid use, particularly if wound healing is a concern.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May lower zinc over time
depletions.alert_body: Long-term corticosteroid use can gradually increase zinc loss through urine over months, which can slow skin healing in some people.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — zinc intake supports skin and immune balance during long-term corticosteroid use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider a multivitamin with zinc if skin healing feels slower than usual.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of zinc include meat, poultry, shellfish, beans, lentils, nuts, seeds, and whole grains.

depletions.mechanism: Corticosteroids alter tryptophan-kynurenine pathway metabolism in a manner similar to estrogens, increasing demand for vitamin B6 as a cofactor. Increased hepatic tryptophan oxygenase activity depletes pyridoxal phosphate.
depletions.clinical_impact: B6 deficiency may contribute to mood changes, impaired immune function, and peripheral neuropathy — all concerns in patients on systemic corticosteroids.
depletions.recommendation: Ensure adequate B6 intake through diet or a B-complex supplement providing 25–50 mg of B6 daily.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May modestly affect vitamin B6 over time
depletions.alert_body: With long-term corticosteroid use, the body's demand for vitamin B6 gradually increases. Levels may drift lower over months for some people.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — B6 intake supports levels during long-term corticosteroid therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider a B-complex if mood changes are noticeable; discuss with your clinician.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin B6 include poultry, fish, potatoes, chickpeas, bananas, and fortified cereals.

depletions.mechanism: SSRIs can cause Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone secretion (SIADH), leading to increased water retention and dilutional hyponatremia. Serotonin directly stimulates ADH release from the hypothalamus.
depletions.clinical_impact: Hyponatremia causes nausea, headache, confusion, seizures, and in severe cases coma. Elderly patients and those on diuretics are at highest risk. Onset is often within the first few weeks of treatment.
depletions.recommendation: If you are elderly or on diuretics and starting an SSRI, sodium levels should be checked within 2–4 weeks. Report symptoms of confusion, nausea, or headache to your doctor promptly. This is a monitoring concern, not a supplementation target.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum sodium at baseline and 2–4 weeks after starting SSRI therapy in elderly patients or those on concurrent diuretics.

sources.label: De Picker L et al. Antidepressants and the risk of hyponatremia: a class-by-class review of literature. Psychosomatics. 2014;55(6):536-47

depletions.alert_headline: May affect sodium early in treatment
depletions.alert_body: In the first weeks of an SSRI, sodium levels can gradually drift lower, especially in older adults or those on water pills. Your prescriber often checks this early.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — your care plan includes sodium awareness, which helps catch changes early.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking for a sodium check 2-4 weeks after starting an SSRI.

depletions.mechanism: Serotonin signaling (via 5-HT2A receptors on osteoclasts) regulates bone remodeling. SSRI-induced chronic elevation of serotonin activity increases osteoclast activation and bone resorption, leading to measurable decreases in bone density over time.
depletions.clinical_impact: Long-term SSRI use is associated with significantly decreased bone mineral density and increased fracture risk, particularly in elderly women. Calcium depletion from bone contributes to this risk.
depletions.recommendation: Ensure adequate calcium intake (1,000–1,200 mg/day) and vitamin D supplementation. Discuss bone density monitoring with your doctor if on long-term SSRI therapy.
depletions.monitoring_note: Consider DEXA scan for bone density assessment in patients on long-term SSRI therapy, especially elderly women.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Calcium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

depletions.alert_headline: May affect bone calcium over years
depletions.alert_body: With long-term SSRI use, bone calcium can gradually shift over years, which may influence bone density — most relevant for older adults.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — calcium intake supports bone health during long-term SSRI therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing bone density screening if on an SSRI long-term.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of calcium include dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens, almonds, and canned sardines or salmon with bones.

depletions.mechanism: The bone density effects of SSRIs increase vitamin D demand for calcium regulation. Additionally, depression itself is associated with vitamin D deficiency, and lifestyle factors in depressed patients (reduced sun exposure, poor diet) may compound the association.
depletions.clinical_impact: Low vitamin D worsens depression outcomes (inverse relationship between vitamin D and depression is well-documented), creates a reinforcing cycle, and compounds SSRI-related bone density effects.
depletions.recommendation: Check your vitamin D level when starting SSRI therapy. Many patients benefit from supplementation with 1,000–2,000 IU vitamin D3 daily to maintain optimal levels.
depletions.monitoring_note: Test serum 25-OH vitamin D at baseline and annually in patients on long-term SSRIs.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May relate to vitamin D over time
depletions.alert_body: Vitamin D often runs lower in people on long-term SSRIs, partly because indoor time tends to increase. Levels can drift lower over months.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin D intake supports overall balance during SSRI therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider checking vitamin D at your annual visit, especially in winter months.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin D include fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk and plant milks, and fortified cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital, primidone) induce hepatic CYP enzymes that accelerate catabolism of 25-hydroxyvitamin D and 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, reducing circulating levels. Non-enzyme-inducers (valproate, lamotrigine) also reduce vitamin D through less-defined mechanisms.
depletions.clinical_impact: Vitamin D deficiency from AEDs causes bone disease (osteomalacia, osteoporosis), increased fracture risk, muscle weakness, and secondary hyperparathyroidism. This is a well-recognized AED complication.
depletions.recommendation: All patients on AEDs should take vitamin D3 supplementation, typically 1,000–2,000 IU daily. Higher doses (up to 4,000 IU) may be needed with enzyme-inducing drugs. Regular 25-OH-D level monitoring is essential.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum 25-OH vitamin D every 6–12 months in all patients on long-term AED therapy. Target 25-OH-D >30 ng/mL.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Vitamin D Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

depletions.alert_headline: Can lower vitamin D with long-term use
depletions.alert_body: Certain seizure medications can gradually speed up how the body breaks down vitamin D over months, leading to lower levels with ongoing use.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin D intake aligns with guidance for long-term seizure-medication use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking for a 25-OH-D check; your neurologist can guide dosing.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin D include fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk and plant milks, and fortified cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Secondary to vitamin D depletion: reduced vitamin D impairs intestinal calcium absorption and increases PTH-driven bone resorption. Phenytoin also directly inhibits intestinal calcium transport independent of vitamin D.
depletions.clinical_impact: Hypocalcemia and metabolic bone disease (anticonvulsant osteomalacia) are well-documented. Risk of fragility fractures is significantly elevated, especially in institutionalized or immobile patients on multiple AEDs.
depletions.recommendation: Supplement with 1,000–1,500 mg calcium daily alongside vitamin D in all patients on long-term AED therapy. Calcium citrate preferred for reliability of absorption.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum calcium and alkaline phosphatase (marker of bone turnover) annually in patients on enzyme-inducing AEDs.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Calcium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

depletions.alert_headline: May lower calcium with long-term use
depletions.alert_body: With long-term use of certain seizure medications, calcium absorption can gradually decline over months — this often moves alongside vitamin D.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — calcium intake supports bone balance during long-term seizure-medication use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing calcium and vitamin D together at your neurology visits.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of calcium include dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens, almonds, and canned sardines or salmon with bones.

depletions.mechanism: Phenytoin, carbamazepine, and valproate impair folate absorption and metabolism through multiple mechanisms: inhibiting intestinal folate conjugase, competing with folate transport, inhibiting dihydrofolate reductase, and accelerating hepatic folate catabolism via CYP induction.
depletions.clinical_impact: Folate deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia, elevated homocysteine, increased cardiovascular risk, and neural tube defects in offspring. Paradoxically, high-dose folate supplementation can reduce phenytoin efficacy, requiring careful management.
depletions.recommendation: Low-dose folic acid (0.4–1 mg/day) is generally recommended for patients on these AEDs. Women of childbearing age should take 4–5 mg/day under medical supervision. Discuss folate supplementation with your neurologist before starting.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum folate and CBC annually. In women of childbearing age, discuss with neurologist regarding folate dose that balances maternal deficiency risk against potential seizure threshold effects.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Folate Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

depletions.alert_headline: May lower folate with long-term use
depletions.alert_body: Several seizure medications can gradually reduce folate over months. Folate matters especially for people planning pregnancy, so medical guidance is important.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — folate intake reflects thoughtful planning during long-term therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing folate dose with your neurologist, especially when planning pregnancy.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of folate include leafy greens, lentils, chickpeas, asparagus, avocado, and fortified grains and cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Some AEDs, particularly valproate and phenytoin, may reduce vitamin B12 absorption or impair its utilization. Enzyme-inducing AEDs may also increase B12 catabolism. Additionally, concurrent folate deficiency can mask B12 deficiency in lab values.
depletions.clinical_impact: B12 deficiency contributes to peripheral neuropathy, cognitive changes, and megaloblastic anemia. Particularly concerning when combined with folate depletion by the same drug class.
depletions.recommendation: Include B12 in routine monitoring for patients on AEDs. A B-complex supplement or sublingual B12 (1,000 mcg/day) can help maintain adequate levels.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum B12 annually in patients on long-term AED therapy, especially valproate and phenytoin.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May lower vitamin B12 over years
depletions.alert_body: Some seizure medications can gradually affect vitamin B12 levels over years. Since they also affect folate, B12 is often checked alongside.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — B12 intake supports levels during long-term seizure-medication use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider including B12 in your routine labs during long-term therapy.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin B12 include fish, meat, poultry, eggs, dairy, and fortified plant milks and cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Enzyme-inducing AEDs (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital) accelerate hepatic metabolism of vitamin K via CYP2C9 induction, reducing vitamin K-dependent clotting factor synthesis. This is critical in pregnant women on these drugs.
depletions.clinical_impact: Neonatal hemorrhagic disease of the newborn is a well-documented risk when mothers take enzyme-inducing AEDs during pregnancy. Maternal bone health is also affected as vitamin K is required for osteocalcin carboxylation.
depletions.recommendation: Pregnant women on enzyme-inducing AEDs require vitamin K supplementation under medical supervision. All patients should ensure adequate dietary vitamin K from green leafy vegetables. Do not self-supplement with high-dose K without medical guidance.
depletions.monitoring_note: Pregnant women on enzyme-inducing AEDs should receive vitamin K1 (phytomenadione) 10–20 mg/day in the last 4 weeks of pregnancy (per clinical guidelines). Discuss with your neurologist and obstetrician.

sources.label: Yerby MS. Management issues for women with epilepsy: neural tube defects and folic acid supplementation. Neurology. 2003

depletions.alert_headline: May affect vitamin K with long-term use
depletions.alert_body: Certain seizure medications can gradually increase vitamin K turnover over months. This matters especially during pregnancy and is managed medically.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin K awareness is part of thoughtful care during long-term therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing vitamin K with your neurologist — self-supplementing isn't recommended.

depletions.mechanism: Valproic acid and carbamazepine impair biotin absorption from the gut, accelerate biotin catabolism via beta-oxidation of the valproyl side chain, and may compete with biotin for intestinal transport. They also impair biotinidase activity needed for biotin recycling.
depletions.clinical_impact: Biotin deficiency causes hair loss, skin rash, neurological symptoms (depression, hallucinations, numbness), and impaired glucose metabolism. Symptoms can mimic other conditions and may be overlooked.
depletions.recommendation: Consider biotin supplementation (5–10 mg/day) for patients on valproate or carbamazepine, especially if experiencing hair thinning or skin changes.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May lower biotin over time
depletions.alert_body: Valproate and carbamazepine can gradually reduce biotin levels with long-term use over months. Some people notice hair thinning or skin changes.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — biotin intake supports hair and skin balance during long-term therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing biotin with your neurologist if hair thinning is noticeable.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of biotin include eggs, salmon, pork, sunflower seeds, almonds, sweet potatoes, and spinach.

depletions.mechanism: Valproic acid (valproate) forms acylcarnitine conjugates that are excreted renally, directly depleting carnitine stores. It also inhibits carnitine biosynthesis and transport. Plasma and tissue carnitine levels can fall significantly with long-term valproate therapy.
depletions.clinical_impact: Carnitine depletion from valproate impairs mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation, contributing to hepatotoxicity (Reye-like syndrome), hyperammonemia, and metabolic encephalopathy. Life-threatening in rare cases, especially in children.
depletions.recommendation: L-carnitine supplementation (50–100 mg/kg/day in children; 1–3 g/day in adults) is recommended for patients on valproate with carnitine deficiency, metabolic symptoms, or liver disease. Discuss with your neurologist.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check plasma carnitine levels in all patients on long-term valproate, especially children, those on multiple AEDs, or those with liver disease.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Carnitine Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

depletions.alert_headline: Valproate may lower carnitine over time
depletions.alert_body: Valproate can gradually reduce carnitine stores over months of use. This effect is medically managed and most relevant for children or people with liver issues.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — carnitine intake aligns with clinical guidance for long-term valproate use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing L-carnitine with your neurologist, especially for children on valproate.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of carnitine include red meat, poultry, fish, and dairy products.

depletions.mechanism: ACE inhibitors chelate zinc ions (zinc is a cofactor at the ACE active site, and the drugs bind it). This binding increases urinary zinc excretion. Captopril in particular has strong zinc-chelating properties due to its sulfhydryl group.
depletions.clinical_impact: Zinc deficiency may contribute to ACE inhibitor-induced side effects including loss of taste (dysgeusia) and cough. Long-term zinc depletion impairs immune function, wound healing, and testosterone levels.
depletions.recommendation: Consider zinc supplementation (10–15 mg/day as zinc glycinate) for patients on long-term ACE inhibitor therapy, especially if experiencing loss of taste or recurrent infections.
depletions.monitoring_note: Consider zinc supplementation trial if patient on ACE inhibitor develops dysgeusia (taste disturbance).

sources.label: NIH ODS — Zinc Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

depletions.alert_headline: May lower zinc over time
depletions.alert_body: ACE inhibitors can gradually increase zinc loss through urine with long-term use over months. Some people notice a blunted sense of taste.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — zinc intake supports taste and immune balance during long-term therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider a modest zinc supplement if taste feels duller than usual.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of zinc include meat, poultry, shellfish, beans, lentils, nuts, seeds, and whole grains.

depletions.mechanism: Beta blockers may inhibit mitochondrial enzymes involved in CoQ10 synthesis and utilization, including NADH-CoQ reductase and succinate-CoQ reductase. Propranolol and metoprolol have been specifically studied. The depletion appears less pronounced than with statins.
depletions.clinical_impact: Reduced CoQ10 may contribute to fatigue, exercise intolerance, and worsened cardiac energy metabolism — effects that compound the intrinsic negative chronotropic/inotropic effects of beta blockers.
depletions.recommendation: Consider 100–200 mg CoQ10 daily if experiencing fatigue or exercise intolerance while on beta blockers. This is particularly relevant for patients with heart failure.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Coenzyme Q10 Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
Folkers K et al. Lovastatin decreases coenzyme Q levels in humans. Proc Natl Acad Sci. 1990

depletions.alert_headline: May lower CoQ10 over time
depletions.alert_body: Beta blockers can gradually reduce how the body produces and uses CoQ10 with long-term use over months. Some people notice lower energy or slower recovery.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — CoQ10 intake supports energy balance during long-term beta-blocker use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing CoQ10 with your cardiologist if fatigue feels persistent.
depletions.food_sources_short: Dietary CoQ10 is minimal — organ meats and fatty fish contain small amounts, but a supplement is usually the practical route.

depletions.mechanism: Melatonin synthesis in the pineal gland requires beta-adrenergic stimulation to activate arylalkylamine N-acetyltransferase (AANAT). Beta blockers block beta-1 and beta-2 adrenergic receptors on pinealocytes, suppressing the nocturnal melatonin surge by up to 50% or more.
depletions.clinical_impact: Melatonin suppression from beta blockers is a common cause of drug-induced insomnia and sleep disturbance. Patients frequently report difficulty falling or staying asleep, which may be misattributed to anxiety or the underlying condition.
depletions.recommendation: If experiencing sleep problems on a beta blocker, discuss with your doctor. Low-dose melatonin (0.5–1 mg) taken 30–60 minutes before bed may help restore sleep quality. Avoid high doses (>5 mg) unless directed.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Melatonin Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

depletions.alert_headline: Can affect nighttime melatonin over time
depletions.alert_body: Beta blockers can gradually reduce the body's nighttime melatonin release with regular use over weeks. Some people notice changes in sleep onset.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — low-dose melatonin aligns with guidance for people using beta blockers long-term.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing low-dose melatonin with your prescriber for sleep support.

depletions.mechanism: Some NSAIDs, particularly high-dose aspirin and sulindac, inhibit dihydrofolate reductase at high concentrations and may displace folate from plasma binding proteins, increasing urinary excretion. Regular NSAID use may impair intestinal folate transport.
depletions.clinical_impact: Mild folate reduction may elevate homocysteine over time, increasing cardiovascular risk. The effect is most clinically significant with chronic high-dose NSAID use.
depletions.recommendation: Ensure adequate folate intake through diet or a daily multivitamin with 400 mcg folate/folic acid if taking NSAIDs regularly. This is particularly important for individuals also on methotrexate.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May slightly lower folate over time
depletions.alert_body: With regular use, some NSAIDs can gradually affect folate metabolism over months. The effect is usually modest unless doses are high.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — folate intake supports levels during regular NSAID use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider a multivitamin with folate if NSAID use is frequent; discuss with your clinician.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of folate include leafy greens, lentils, chickpeas, asparagus, avocado, and fortified grains and cereals.

depletions.mechanism: NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, which reduces the protective mucous layer of the GI tract, leading to mucosal erosion, ulceration, and occult or overt GI bleeding. Chronic blood loss depletes iron stores. Even small amounts of daily bleeding accumulate over time.
depletions.clinical_impact: Chronic NSAID-induced GI bleeding is a leading cause of iron deficiency anemia in older adults. The bleeding may be occult (no visible blood in stool) and go undetected for months while iron stores are depleted.
depletions.recommendation: If taking NSAIDs regularly, watch for signs of iron deficiency (fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath) and ask your doctor to check a CBC and ferritin periodically. Using a proton pump inhibitor with NSAIDs reduces GI risk.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check CBC and serum ferritin annually in patients on regular NSAID therapy. Fecal occult blood testing may be appropriate.

sources.label: Lanas A, Chan FK. Peptic ulcer disease. Lancet. 2017;390:613-624

depletions.alert_headline: May lower iron over time
depletions.alert_body: With long-term NSAID use, small amounts of gut bleeding can gradually reduce iron stores over months. This may not be visible and is often caught on routine labs.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — iron awareness is helpful during long-term NSAID use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking for periodic ferritin and CBC labs if NSAID use is long-term.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of iron include red meat, poultry, seafood, beans, lentils, tofu, spinach, and fortified cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Aspirin and other salicylates compete with vitamin C for renal tubular reabsorption, increasing urinary vitamin C excretion. Aspirin also increases leukocyte turnover (cells rich in vitamin C) and promotes oxidative conditions that consume ascorbate.
depletions.clinical_impact: Chronic aspirin use can cause vitamin C depletion that may impair antioxidant defenses, collagen synthesis, and immune function. This is particularly relevant for elderly patients on daily low-dose aspirin who also have marginal dietary vitamin C intake.
depletions.recommendation: Ensure daily vitamin C intake of 250–500 mg through diet or supplementation when taking aspirin regularly. Take vitamin C separately from aspirin to minimize competition.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: Aspirin may lower vitamin C over time
depletions.alert_body: With regular aspirin use, the body loses more vitamin C through urine over months. The effect is most noticeable with daily low-dose aspirin.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — vitamin C intake supports levels during regular aspirin use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider taking vitamin C at a different time of day from aspirin.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin C include citrus fruits, strawberries, bell peppers, broccoli, kiwi, and tomatoes.
