depletions.mechanism: Gut bacteria synthesize modest amounts of B vitamins including B12, biotin, folate, riboflavin, and pantothenic acid. Broad-spectrum antibiotics disrupt the intestinal microbiome, transiently reducing bacterial B vitamin synthesis. The primary B vitamin source is dietary, so the impact is most significant in individuals with marginal dietary intake.
depletions.clinical_impact: Short-course antibiotics rarely cause clinically significant B12 depletion in well-nourished individuals. The effect is more concerning with repeated or prolonged antibiotic courses, in elderly patients, and those with inadequate dietary B vitamin intake.
depletions.recommendation: After antibiotic use, restore gut flora with probiotic-rich foods (yogurt, kefir, fermented vegetables) or a high-quality probiotic supplement for 4–8 weeks. A B-complex supplement during and after antibiotic courses provides a reasonable safety net.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May briefly affect B vitamins with regular use
depletions.alert_body: Broad-spectrum antibiotics can gradually affect gut-bacteria production of some B vitamins during a course. The effect is usually short-lived and reverses after treatment.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — a B-complex is a reasonable companion during and after antibiotic treatment.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider a B-complex and fermented foods during and after a course of antibiotics.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of B vitamins include meat, poultry, fish, eggs, dairy, leafy greens, legumes, and fortified grains.
depletions.citation_review_note: drug_ref.id 'antibiotics_broadspectrum' is a synthetic non-RxCUI identifier; the app matches medications by numeric RxCUI, so this rule can never fire. Suppressed (needs_revision) pending Section 8 conversion to class:broad_spectrum_antibiotics with real member rxcuis. The mechanism/citation are sound — the defect is the drug identifier, not the evidence.

depletions.mechanism: Intestinal bacteria (particularly gram-positive anaerobes like Bacteroides and Prevotella) synthesize menaquinone (vitamin K2), which contributes to vitamin K status. Antibiotics that disrupt these populations reduce endogenous vitamin K2 synthesis. This effect is most significant for individuals on warfarin or with marginal dietary vitamin K1 intake.
depletions.clinical_impact: Antibiotic-induced vitamin K reduction can potentiate anticoagulation in warfarin users, leading to dangerously elevated INR and bleeding risk. In non-anticoagulated patients the effect is generally modest.
depletions.recommendation: Patients on warfarin must have their INR monitored closely during and after any antibiotic course. Non-anticoagulated patients should ensure adequate dietary vitamin K from green leafy vegetables and consider a low-dose K2 supplement (100 mcg MK-7) after antibiotic courses.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check INR within 3–5 days of starting antibiotics in all warfarin-treated patients. Antibiotics frequently destabilize anticoagulation control.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: Can affect vitamin K during a course
depletions.alert_body: Broad-spectrum antibiotics can gradually reduce gut-bacteria vitamin K production during a course. This matters most for people on warfarin and is managed medically.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin K awareness is helpful during and after antibiotic courses.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: If you take warfarin, ask about INR checks during and after an antibiotic course.
depletions.citation_review_note: drug_ref.id 'antibiotics_broadspectrum' is a synthetic non-RxCUI identifier; the app matches medications by numeric RxCUI, so this rule can never fire. Suppressed (needs_revision) pending Section 8 conversion to class:broad_spectrum_antibiotics with real member rxcuis. The mechanism/citation are sound — the defect is the drug identifier, not the evidence.

depletions.mechanism: Calcium supplements and calcium-rich foods bind levothyroxine in the gastrointestinal tract through a direct physicochemical interaction, reducing drug absorption rather than depleting body calcium stores. Levothyroxine adsorbs to calcium carbonate in an acidic environment, lowering its bioavailability.
depletions.clinical_impact: Taking calcium together with levothyroxine reduces thyroid hormone absorption and can raise TSH into the underactive range — in a pharmacokinetic study, levothyroxine uptake fell from about 84% to 58% when taken with a large (2 g) calcium dose. The result can be inadequate thyroid control and hypothyroid symptoms.
depletions.recommendation: Take levothyroxine at least 4 hours apart from calcium supplements or calcium-rich meals. If you start, stop, or change calcium timing, ask your clinician about checking your thyroid levels.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check TSH 6–8 weeks after any change to calcium supplement timing or dose. Annual bone density assessment if on long-term thyroid replacement, especially postmenopausal women.

sources.label: Singh N et al. Effect of calcium carbonate on the absorption of levothyroxine. JAMA. 2000;283(21):2822-5
Singh N et al. The acute effect of calcium carbonate on the intestinal absorption of levothyroxine. Thyroid. 2001;11(10):967-71

depletions.alert_headline: Calcium can reduce thyroid-med absorption
depletions.alert_body: Calcium taken near your thyroid medication can gradually reduce how much of the dose is absorbed over weeks. Separating them by time usually solves it.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — calcium intake supports bone health; timing separately keeps your thyroid dose steady.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider taking levothyroxine at least 4 hours apart from calcium-rich meals or supplements.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of calcium include dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens, almonds, and canned sardines or salmon with bones.
depletions.citation_review_note: Ca–levothyroxine absorption interaction is well-documented (Singh 2000 JAMA, 2001 Thyroid); overstated 40% figure corrected to the study values and out-of-scope bone-calcium claims removed.

depletions.mechanism: Iron supplements form an insoluble complex with levothyroxine in the gut, reducing levothyroxine absorption. This is a drug-nutrient interaction affecting drug bioavailability rather than a direct iron depletion; in a controlled trial, taking ferrous sulfate with levothyroxine raised TSH from 1.6 to 5.4 mU/L over 12 weeks.
depletions.clinical_impact: Co-administration can lead to inadequate thyroid hormone levels and a return of hypothyroid symptoms; the effect is variable but clinically significant in some patients.
depletions.recommendation: Take iron supplements at least 4 hours before or after levothyroxine. If you start or stop iron, ask your clinician about rechecking your thyroid levels.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check TSH within 6–8 weeks of starting iron supplementation in levothyroxine-treated patients. Separate administration times by at least 4 hours.

sources.label: Campbell NR et al. Ferrous sulfate reduces thyroxine efficacy in patients with hypothyroidism. Ann Intern Med. 1992;117(12):1010-3

depletions.alert_headline: Iron can reduce thyroid-med absorption
depletions.alert_body: Iron taken near your thyroid medication can gradually reduce absorption of the dose over weeks. Separating them by several hours usually solves it.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — iron intake supports overall balance; timing separately keeps your thyroid dose steady.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider taking iron at least 4 hours apart from your levothyroxine dose.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of iron include red meat, poultry, seafood, beans, lentils, tofu, spinach, and fortified cereals.
depletions.citation_review_note: Placeholder NIH-ODS Iron sheet replaced with the primary controlled trial (Campbell 1992); unsupported 30–45% figure and out-of-scope claims (gastric acid, thyroperoxidase) removed.

depletions.mechanism: Atypical antipsychotics promote weight gain and metabolic syndrome, which are associated with reduced vitamin D bioavailability (vitamin D is sequestered in adipose tissue). Sedation and reduced outdoor activity decrease sun exposure and cutaneous vitamin D synthesis.
depletions.clinical_impact: Vitamin D deficiency is highly prevalent in patients on antipsychotics and is associated with metabolic syndrome, depression, immune dysfunction, and bone loss — all already increased by antipsychotic therapy.
depletions.recommendation: Check vitamin D levels at baseline and annually in all patients on antipsychotic medications. Supplement with vitamin D3 1,000–2,000 IU daily, adjusting dose based on blood levels.
depletions.monitoring_note: Test serum 25-OH vitamin D at baseline and every 12 months. Target >30 ng/mL.

sources.label: Fernandes BS et al. Vitamin D levels in schizophrenia and first-episode psychosis. Schizophr Res. 2015

depletions.alert_headline: May lower vitamin D over time
depletions.alert_body: Vitamin D often runs lower with long-term antipsychotic use over months, partly due to less sun exposure and metabolic shifts.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin D intake supports overall balance during long-term therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider an annual 25-OH-D check during long-term antipsychotic therapy.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin D include fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk and plant milks, and fortified cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Several antipsychotic drugs have been shown to inhibit mitochondrial Complex I activity and reduce CoQ10-dependent electron transport chain function. This may contribute to antipsychotic-induced metabolic side effects and fatigue.
depletions.clinical_impact: Reduced CoQ10 availability may worsen antipsychotic-induced fatigue and contribute to the metabolic and mitochondrial dysfunction increasingly recognized with these medications.
depletions.recommendation: Consider 100–200 mg CoQ10 daily if experiencing marked fatigue or metabolic side effects while on antipsychotic therapy.

sources.label: Rezin GT et al. Inhibition of mitochondrial respiratory chain in brain of rats subjected to an experimental model of depression. Neurochem Int. 2008

depletions.alert_headline: May lower CoQ10 over time
depletions.alert_body: Some antipsychotics can gradually affect how cells produce energy from CoQ10 over months. A few people notice lower energy with long-term use.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — CoQ10 intake supports energy balance during long-term therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing CoQ10 with your prescriber if fatigue feels persistent.
depletions.food_sources_short: Dietary CoQ10 is minimal — organ meats and fatty fish contain small amounts, but a supplement is usually the practical route.

depletions.mechanism: Stimulants increase sympathetic nervous system activity and catecholamine release, which promote urinary magnesium excretion. Appetite suppression (a common stimulant side effect) also reduces dietary magnesium intake.
depletions.clinical_impact: Magnesium deficiency exacerbates hyperactivity, impulsivity, anxiety, and sleep disturbance — the very symptoms being treated in ADHD patients. Low magnesium also worsens insomnia, a common complaint with stimulant therapy.
depletions.recommendation: Consider magnesium glycinate supplementation (100–300 mg daily, taken in the evening to support sleep) in patients on stimulant medications. Foods rich in magnesium include dark chocolate, nuts, seeds, and leafy greens.

sources.label: Kozielec T, Starobrat-Hermelin B. Assessment of magnesium levels in children with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Magnes Res. 1997

depletions.alert_headline: May lower magnesium over time
depletions.alert_body: ADHD stimulants can gradually increase magnesium turnover with regular use over months. Reduced appetite from the medication can also trim dietary intake.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — magnesium intake supports calm and sleep balance during stimulant therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider magnesium in the evening; discuss with your prescriber if sleep is restless.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of magnesium include leafy greens, nuts, seeds, whole grains, beans, and dark chocolate.

depletions.mechanism: Acidic urine (created by vitamin C supplementation) increases renal excretion of amphetamines by ion trapping, effectively reducing their bioavailability and duration of action. Conversely, vitamin C is not depleted by stimulants, but the interaction can reduce stimulant efficacy. This is an absorption/efficacy interaction rather than a true depletion.
depletions.clinical_impact: Taking high-dose vitamin C within 1–2 hours of amphetamine-based medications can reduce drug absorption and efficacy by up to 50% via pH-dependent ion trapping in the gut and accelerated renal elimination.
depletions.recommendation: Avoid taking large doses of vitamin C (>250 mg) within 1–2 hours before or after taking amphetamine-based ADHD medications. This helps maintain consistent medication levels and effectiveness.

sources.label: Meltzer PC et al. Medications used in ADHD: pharmacokinetic considerations. Clinical Pharmacokinetics. 2000

depletions.alert_headline: Vitamin C timing can affect stimulant dose
depletions.alert_body: High-dose vitamin C taken near an amphetamine-based stimulant can gradually affect how well the dose works over weeks. Separating them by time usually resolves it.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin C supports overall balance; timing separately keeps your medication steady.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider taking high-dose vitamin C at least 1-2 hours apart from stimulant doses.

depletions.mechanism: HIV protease inhibitors, particularly ritonavir-boosted regimens, inhibit CYP3A4-mediated vitamin D activation and may also directly impair 25-hydroxylation of vitamin D in the liver. HIV infection itself and immune activation independently reduce vitamin D levels.
depletions.clinical_impact: Vitamin D deficiency is nearly universal in HIV patients on protease inhibitors and contributes to bone disease (osteopenia, osteoporosis), immune dysfunction, increased infection risk, and metabolic complications including insulin resistance.
depletions.recommendation: Vitamin D supplementation (1,000–4,000 IU daily) is widely recommended for HIV patients on ART. Levels should be monitored and dosing adjusted to achieve 25-OH-D >30 ng/mL.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check 25-OH vitamin D every 6–12 months in all HIV-positive patients on protease inhibitor-based regimens.

sources.label: Dao CN et al. Low vitamin D among HIV-infected adults: prevalence of and risk factors for low vitamin D levels in a cohort of HIV-infected adults. Medicine. 2011

depletions.alert_headline: May lower vitamin D with long-term use
depletions.alert_body: HIV protease inhibitors can gradually affect how the body activates vitamin D over months. Levels often need active management during therapy.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin D intake aligns with standard care during long-term ART.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider regular 25-OH-D checks; your HIV clinician can guide targets.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin D include fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk and plant milks, and fortified cereals.

depletions.mechanism: HIV infection causes systemic zinc depletion through increased urinary losses, impaired intestinal absorption, and redistribution of zinc as part of the acute phase response. Protease inhibitors may compound this through GI side effects that reduce dietary zinc absorption.
depletions.clinical_impact: Zinc deficiency is associated with impaired immune function, increased HIV disease progression, poor wound healing, and diarrhea — a vicious cycle given that diarrhea (common with some antiretrovirals) further depletes zinc.
depletions.recommendation: Zinc supplementation (10–15 mg/day as zinc glycinate) is frequently recommended in HIV patients. However, excessive zinc can be immunosuppressive — do not exceed 40 mg/day without medical supervision.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum zinc levels annually in HIV-positive patients, especially those with recurrent infections or diarrhea.

sources.label: Baum MK et al. Micronutrients in HIV-infected individuals. HIV Clin Trials. 2003

depletions.alert_headline: May lower zinc over time
depletions.alert_body: With long-term HIV protease inhibitor use, zinc can gradually run lower over months. This often pairs with broader nutrition support in HIV care.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — zinc intake supports immune balance during long-term ART.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing a modest zinc supplement with your HIV clinician.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of zinc include meat, poultry, shellfish, beans, lentils, nuts, seeds, and whole grains.

depletions.mechanism: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1), blocking the recycling of vitamin K from its epoxide form back to the active hydroquinone form. This functional depletion of active vitamin K prevents carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X, as well as proteins C and S. This is the intended pharmacological mechanism.
depletions.clinical_impact: While the depletion of active vitamin K is the therapeutic goal of warfarin, it also depletes vitamin K-dependent proteins involved in bone metabolism (osteocalcin, matrix Gla protein), potentially contributing to vascular calcification and osteoporosis with long-term use.
depletions.recommendation: Do NOT supplement with additional vitamin K without medical guidance — it will reduce warfarin's anticoagulant effect. Instead, maintain a consistent daily vitamin K intake from diet (green leafy vegetables). Sudden changes in vitamin K intake (including starting/stopping supplements) destabilize INR control.
depletions.monitoring_note: INR must be monitored regularly (typically every 4 weeks when stable). Any change in vitamin K-rich food consumption, antibiotic use, or new medications requires prompt INR re-check within 3–5 days.

sources.label: Hirsh J et al. Oral anticoagulants: mechanism of action, clinical effectiveness, and optimal therapeutic range. Chest. 2001

depletions.alert_headline: Warfarin changes how vitamin K works
depletions.alert_body: Warfarin works by blocking vitamin K's role in clotting — this is long-term by design. Changes in vitamin K intake can shift your INR over weeks.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — steady vitamin K intake supports consistent INR during warfarin therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider keeping your vitamin K intake steady day to day; discuss changes with your prescriber.

depletions.mechanism: Some MAOIs, particularly hydrazine-based compounds (phenelzine), form hydrazones with pyridoxal phosphate (the active form of vitamin B6), inactivating it and promoting its urinary excretion. This mechanism is similar to isoniazid-induced B6 deficiency.
depletions.clinical_impact: B6 deficiency from hydrazine MAOIs can cause peripheral neuropathy, dermatitis, stomatitis, and mood disturbances. Phenelzine-induced B6 deficiency presenting as peripheral neuropathy is a recognized clinical problem.
depletions.recommendation: Patients on phenelzine or other hydrazine-class MAOIs should take vitamin B6 supplementation (50–100 mg/day as pyridoxine) to prevent neuropathy. Discuss timing and dose with your prescribing psychiatrist.
depletions.monitoring_note: Monitor for signs of peripheral neuropathy (numbness, tingling in hands/feet) in patients on phenelzine. Check plasma pyridoxal phosphate if neuropathy develops.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Vitamin B6 Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: Phenelzine may lower vitamin B6 over time
depletions.alert_body: Hydrazine-class MAOIs like phenelzine can gradually reduce active vitamin B6 with long-term use over months. Some people notice tingling or skin changes.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — B6 intake aligns with guidance for long-term phenelzine-class MAOI use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing a B6 supplement with your prescribing psychiatrist.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin B6 include poultry, fish, potatoes, chickpeas, bananas, and fortified cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Type 2 diabetes itself is associated with hypomagnesemia through insulin resistance, glycosuria-induced osmotic diuresis, and altered renal handling of magnesium. Some diabetes medications (particularly insulin secretagogues) may compound this through improved glycemic control and resulting osmotic changes.
depletions.clinical_impact: Low magnesium in diabetic patients worsens insulin resistance, increases cardiovascular risk, and impairs glucose metabolism — potentially creating a worsening cycle. It also contributes to hypertension and neuropathy.
depletions.recommendation: Magnesium supplementation (200–400 mg/day as glycinate or citrate) has shown benefits in improving insulin sensitivity and glycemic control in type 2 diabetes. Discuss with your diabetes care team.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum magnesium periodically in all type 2 diabetic patients, particularly those with poor glycemic control or cardiovascular risk factors.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Magnesium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
NIH ODS — Magnesium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals

depletions.alert_headline: May lower magnesium over time
depletions.alert_body: Type 2 diabetes itself and some of its medications can gradually lower magnesium over months. Keeping magnesium steady often supports blood-sugar balance.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — magnesium intake supports blood-sugar and overall balance during diabetes therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing magnesium with your diabetes care team at routine visits.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of magnesium include leafy greens, nuts, seeds, whole grains, beans, and dark chocolate.

depletions.mechanism: Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) directly inhibit renal tubular magnesium reabsorption by downregulating TRPM6/TRPM7 channels, leading to profound renal magnesium wasting. This is a recognized adverse effect of these immunosuppressants.
depletions.clinical_impact: Severe hypomagnesemia from calcineurin inhibitors causes muscle cramps, tremors, cardiac arrhythmias, and neurotoxicity (which can mimic or worsen the neurotoxicity of tacrolimus itself). Post-transplant hypomagnesemia is highly prevalent.
depletions.recommendation: Magnesium supplementation is routinely required in transplant patients on calcineurin inhibitors. Oral magnesium oxide, glycinate, or citrate (400–800 mg/day in divided doses) is typically prescribed. IV magnesium may be needed for severe deficiency.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum magnesium weekly in early post-transplant period, then monthly when stable. Target serum magnesium >0.7 mmol/L (1.7 mg/dL).

sources.label: NIH ODS — Magnesium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
Navaneethan SD et al. Hypomagnesemia in kidney transplant recipients. Transplantation. 2006

depletions.alert_headline: Can lower magnesium with regular use
depletions.alert_body: Calcineurin inhibitors like tacrolimus and cyclosporine can gradually increase magnesium loss through urine over weeks. Magnesium is typically monitored closely in transplant care.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — magnesium intake is part of standard care during calcineurin-inhibitor therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Ask your transplant team about your magnesium schedule — this is a routine part of care.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of magnesium include leafy greens, nuts, seeds, whole grains, beans, and dark chocolate.

depletions.mechanism: Combined immunosuppressive regimens (often including steroids + calcineurin inhibitors) impair vitamin D via multiple pathways: accelerated hepatic catabolism by corticosteroids, reduced sun exposure from photosensitivity, impaired renal 1-alpha hydroxylation, and reduced dietary intake from medication-induced GI side effects.
depletions.clinical_impact: Vitamin D deficiency after organ transplant contributes to osteoporosis, increased fracture risk, immune dysregulation, and cardiovascular complications. Post-transplant bone disease is a major cause of morbidity.
depletions.recommendation: Vitamin D3 supplementation (1,000–4,000 IU/day) is standard of care in transplant patients. Optimal 25-OH-D targets and dosing should be managed by your transplant team.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check 25-OH vitamin D every 3–6 months in the first year post-transplant, then every 6–12 months thereafter.

sources.label: Evenepoel P et al. Bone and mineral metabolism in kidney transplant recipients. Nat Rev Nephrol. 2009

depletions.alert_headline: Can lower vitamin D long-term
depletions.alert_body: Combined immunosuppressive therapy can gradually reduce vitamin D over months. Vitamin D is part of routine labs after transplant and is actively managed.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin D intake is standard of care during transplant follow-up.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Ask your transplant team about your vitamin D target and dosing plan.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin D include fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk and plant milks, and fortified cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Calcium channel blockers may increase urinary potassium excretion through renal tubular effects on potassium secretion channels. The effect is generally mild compared to diuretics and is not consistently demonstrated across all agents in this class.
depletions.clinical_impact: The potassium-wasting effect is generally clinically minor unless combined with other potassium-depleting medications (loop/thiazide diuretics, corticosteroids). Monitoring is most important in patients on combination antihypertensive regimens.
depletions.recommendation: Ensure adequate dietary potassium intake. Check electrolytes if on combination antihypertensive therapy including both CCBs and diuretics.
depletions.monitoring_note: Monitor serum electrolytes in patients on multiple antihypertensive agents.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May slightly affect potassium over time
depletions.alert_body: Calcium channel blockers can gradually shift potassium levels with long-term use over months. The effect is usually mild unless combined with other blood-pressure medications.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — potassium awareness is helpful, and your care plan tracks levels.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking for electrolyte checks if you're on combination blood-pressure therapy.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of potassium include bananas, oranges, potatoes, beans, yogurt, and tomatoes.

depletions.mechanism: Several antihypertensive drug classes increase urinary zinc excretion. This is most documented with ACE inhibitors (via zinc chelation) and thiazide diuretics (via increased urinary volume). The combined effect of multi-drug antihypertensive regimens may produce clinically significant zinc depletion.
depletions.clinical_impact: Zinc deficiency impairs taste acuity (contributing to poor dietary compliance), immune function, wound healing, and sexual function — all concerns in the hypertensive patient population, which tends to be older.
depletions.recommendation: Patients on multiple antihypertensive medications should ensure adequate dietary zinc or consider a modest zinc supplement (10 mg/day as zinc glycinate).

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May slightly lower zinc over time
depletions.alert_body: Several blood-pressure medications can gradually increase urinary zinc loss with long-term use over months. Older adults on multi-drug regimens feel this most.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — zinc intake supports taste and immune balance during long-term therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider a modest zinc supplement; discuss with your prescriber if taste feels duller.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of zinc include meat, poultry, shellfish, beans, lentils, nuts, seeds, and whole grains.

depletions.mechanism: Hydralazine (a vasodilating antihypertensive) has been shown to deplete CoQ10 through uncertain mechanisms, possibly involving oxidative stress generation or inhibition of CoQ10 biosynthetic enzymes. This depletion may blunt its antihypertensive efficacy.
depletions.clinical_impact: CoQ10 supplementation has demonstrated blood pressure-lowering properties in clinical trials, and depletion may partially undermine treatment. Fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance are additional concerns.
depletions.recommendation: Consider 100–200 mg CoQ10 daily if on hydralazine-class antihypertensives, particularly if experiencing fatigue or suboptimal blood pressure control.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: Hydralazine may lower CoQ10 over time
depletions.alert_body: Hydralazine-class blood-pressure medications can gradually lower CoQ10 over months of use. Some people notice lower energy or slower exercise recovery.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — CoQ10 intake supports energy balance during long-term therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing CoQ10 with your prescriber if fatigue feels persistent.
depletions.food_sources_short: Dietary CoQ10 is minimal — organ meats and fatty fish contain small amounts, but a supplement is usually the practical route.

depletions.mechanism: Insulin promotes intracellular uptake of magnesium in muscle and adipose tissue, transiently lowering serum magnesium. In type 1 and insulin-treated type 2 diabetes, urinary magnesium losses are also elevated due to osmotic effects of glycosuria when control is suboptimal.
depletions.clinical_impact: Hypomagnesemia worsens insulin resistance, impairs insulin secretion, and increases cardiovascular risk — all concerns in patients dependent on insulin for glycemic control.
depletions.recommendation: Ensure adequate magnesium intake (400 mg/day from food and/or supplements). Patients with type 1 diabetes should have magnesium levels monitored as part of routine care.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check serum magnesium at least annually in insulin-treated diabetic patients.

sources.label: NIH ODS — Magnesium Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
Barbagallo M, Dominguez LJ. Magnesium and type 2 diabetes. World J Diabetes. 2015

depletions.alert_headline: May lower magnesium over time
depletions.alert_body: Insulin moves magnesium into cells, which can gradually shift measured levels lower over months. Keeping magnesium steady often supports blood-sugar balance.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — magnesium intake supports blood-sugar and overall balance during insulin therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking about magnesium as part of your routine diabetes labs.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of magnesium include leafy greens, nuts, seeds, whole grains, beans, and dark chocolate.

depletions.mechanism: Barbiturates and long-acting benzodiazepines induce hepatic CYP enzymes that accelerate vitamin D catabolism. Sedation from these medications also reduces physical activity and outdoor sun exposure, further limiting cutaneous vitamin D synthesis.
depletions.clinical_impact: Chronic sedative use is associated with falls and fractures in elderly patients — vitamin D deficiency compounds fracture risk by reducing bone density and increasing fall propensity through muscle weakness.
depletions.recommendation: Ensure adequate vitamin D supplementation (1,000–2,000 IU daily) in patients on chronic sedative therapy, especially the elderly. Exercise programs to maintain muscle strength are also important.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check 25-OH vitamin D annually in elderly patients on chronic sedative therapy.

sources.label: Pelton R et al. Drug-Induced Nutrient Depletion Handbook. 2nd ed. Lexi-Comp, 2001

depletions.alert_headline: May lower vitamin D over time
depletions.alert_body: With long-term sedative or sleep-medication use, vitamin D can gradually run lower over months, partly from reduced daytime outdoor activity.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin D intake supports bone and muscle balance during long-term therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider an annual 25-OH-D check and a daytime walk routine where possible.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of vitamin D include fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk and plant milks, and fortified cereals.

depletions.mechanism: Benzodiazepines alter circadian rhythm regulation by modulating GABA-A receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus (the brain's master clock), which suppresses normal nighttime melatonin secretion. This disrupts the natural sleep-wake cycle architecture.
depletions.clinical_impact: Benzodiazepine-induced suppression of melatonin disrupts the natural sleep architecture, contributing to dependence, rebound insomnia on discontinuation, and long-term circadian disruption. This is separate from the drug's direct sedative effect.
depletions.recommendation: Discuss with your doctor about long-term use of benzodiazepines for sleep. Low-dose melatonin (0.5–1 mg taken 30–60 minutes before bed) may help normalize sleep architecture and support tapering strategies. Do not stop benzodiazepines abruptly.

sources.label: Monteleone P et al. Suppression of nocturnal plasma melatonin levels by evening administration of diazepam in humans. Life Sci. 1989

depletions.alert_headline: Can affect nighttime melatonin over time
depletions.alert_body: Benzodiazepines can gradually disrupt the body's nighttime melatonin rhythm with regular use over weeks. Some people notice less restorative sleep.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Nice — low-dose melatonin aligns with guidance for people using benzodiazepines.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider discussing low-dose melatonin and sleep strategy with your prescriber.

depletions.mechanism: High-dose folic acid supplementation may compete with zinc for intestinal absorption via shared transport mechanisms. This interaction is most relevant when very high folic acid doses (>5 mg/day) are taken without balanced mineral supplementation.
depletions.clinical_impact: In populations with already marginal zinc intake (elderly, pregnant women), high folic acid could theoretically reduce zinc absorption enough to impair immune function and growth outcomes. Clinical significance at typical supplement doses (400–800 mcg) is low.
depletions.recommendation: At standard supplemental doses, this interaction is not clinically significant. For those taking high-dose folic acid (>5 mg/day), ensure zinc intake from dietary sources or a balanced multivitamin.

sources.label: Milne DB et al. Folate supplementation and zinc absorption. J Nutr. 1990

depletions.alert_headline: High-dose folate may affect zinc uptake
depletions.alert_body: Taking very high folic acid doses over months may gradually compete with zinc uptake in the gut. At typical multivitamin doses, this effect is minimal.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — your zinc intake supports balance alongside regular folate use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider a balanced multivitamin rather than high single-nutrient doses; check with your clinician.
depletions.food_sources_short: Food sources of zinc include meat, poultry, shellfish, beans, lentils, nuts, seeds, and whole grains.

depletions.mechanism: Isoniazid binds and inactivates pyridoxal phosphate, the active coenzyme form of vitamin B6, and increases urinary loss of pyridoxine metabolites. This can lower functional B6 status during tuberculosis therapy.
depletions.clinical_impact: Low vitamin B6 during isoniazid therapy can contribute to peripheral neuropathy, tingling, burning sensations, and rarely anemia or neuropsychiatric symptoms. Higher-risk patients are often supplemented from the start.
depletions.recommendation: Pyridoxine is commonly used with isoniazid, especially in people at higher neuropathy risk. Confirm the exact dose with the clinician managing tuberculosis therapy.
depletions.monitoring_note: Ask about pyridoxine co-therapy if isoniazid is used long term or if neuropathy symptoms appear.

sources.label: van der Watt JJ et al. Polyneuropathy, anti-tuberculosis treatment and the role of pyridoxine in the HIV/AIDS era: a systematic review. Int J Tuberc Lung Dis. 2011;15(6):722-8
DailyMed — ISONIAZID prescribing information

depletions.alert_headline: May lower vitamin B6 over time
depletions.alert_body: Isoniazid can gradually lower active vitamin B6 over months, which is why clinicians often pair it with pyridoxine during TB treatment.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin B6 intake supports balance during isoniazid therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking your TB clinician whether pyridoxine is already included in your plan.

depletions.mechanism: Methotrexate is an antifolate drug that inhibits folate-dependent metabolic pathways, including dihydrofolate reductase-related recycling. In low-dose inflammatory disease treatment, folic acid is often used to reduce folate-mediated side effects.
depletions.clinical_impact: Insufficient folate support during methotrexate therapy can contribute to mouth sores, nausea, liver-enzyme elevations, cytopenias, and treatment intolerance. The right schedule depends on the methotrexate regimen.
depletions.recommendation: Folic acid or folinic acid support should be clinician-directed with methotrexate. Do not change the folate schedule without the prescriber, because timing and dose can matter.
depletions.monitoring_note: Review folate schedule, CBC, and liver-enzyme monitoring with the methotrexate prescriber.

sources.label: Morgan SL, Baggott JE, Alarcon GS. Methotrexate in rheumatoid arthritis: folate supplementation should always be given. BioDrugs. 1997;8(3):164-75
Shea B et al. Folic acid and folinic acid for reducing side effects in patients receiving methotrexate for rheumatoid arthritis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2013;(5):CD000951

depletions.alert_headline: Methotrexate changes folate pathways
depletions.alert_body: Methotrexate works on folate-dependent pathways over time, so folic acid is often managed as part of the treatment plan rather than added casually.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — folate intake is worth tracking while your clinician manages methotrexate therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider confirming your exact folic-acid schedule with the clinician who prescribes methotrexate.

depletions.mechanism: Orlistat inhibits gastrointestinal lipases and reduces absorption of dietary fat. Because Vitamin A is fat-soluble, lower fat absorption can reduce uptake unless vitamin intake is separated from the orlistat dose.
depletions.clinical_impact: Lower Vitamin A absorption can matter during long-term orlistat use, especially when dietary intake is marginal or when other conditions already affect fat absorption.
depletions.recommendation: Use a multivitamin containing vitamins A, D, E, and K at least 2 hours before or after orlistat, following the product label and clinician advice.
depletions.monitoring_note: Check Vitamin A status if orlistat is used long term or if fat-soluble vitamin intake is low.

sources.label: DailyMed — XENICAL (orlistat) prescribing information

depletions.alert_headline: May lower vitamin A absorption
depletions.alert_body: Orlistat can gradually reduce absorption of Vitamin A over months because this vitamin depends on dietary fat for uptake. Timing a multivitamin away from the dose helps.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin A intake helps cover the fat-soluble vitamin gap during orlistat use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider taking your multivitamin at least 2 hours apart from orlistat.

depletions.mechanism: Sulfasalazine can inhibit intestinal absorption and metabolism of folic acid. This can lower functional folate availability during long-term treatment, especially when baseline folate intake is marginal.
depletions.clinical_impact: Lower folate status can contribute to mouth soreness, anemia, elevated homocysteine, and pregnancy-related neural tube risk. This matters most with long-term therapy or pregnancy planning.
depletions.recommendation: Folate support should be clinician-directed with sulfasalazine, especially before pregnancy or during long-term use. Confirm the form and dose with the prescriber.
depletions.monitoring_note: Review folate status and pregnancy planning needs with the clinician managing sulfasalazine.

sources.label: DailyMed — SULFASALAZINE prescribing information

depletions.alert_headline: May lower folate over time
depletions.alert_body: Sulfasalazine can gradually reduce folate absorption and metabolism over months, so folate support is often worth reviewing during long-term use.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — folate intake supports balance during sulfasalazine therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider reviewing folate needs with your clinician, especially before pregnancy.

depletions.mechanism: Colchicine can affect intestinal mucosal function and has been listed among medicines associated with vitamin B12 malabsorption. Long-term use may reduce B12 uptake in susceptible people.
depletions.clinical_impact: Lower B12 status over time can contribute to anemia, neuropathy symptoms, fatigue, and cognitive changes. Risk is higher when other B12 absorption risks are present.
depletions.recommendation: Consider periodic B12 monitoring during long-term colchicine therapy, especially if neuropathy symptoms, anemia, or additional B12 risk factors are present.
depletions.monitoring_note: Ask about B12 testing if colchicine is used chronically or if neuropathy symptoms develop.

sources.label: DailyMed — Cyanocobalamin label, B12 malabsorption causes
Webb DI et al. Mechanism of vitamin B12 malabsorption in patients receiving colchicine. N Engl J Med. 1968;279(16):845-50

depletions.alert_headline: May affect B12 absorption over time
depletions.alert_body: With long-term colchicine use, vitamin B12 absorption can gradually become less reliable over months, especially when other B12 risks are present.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin B12 intake supports balance during long-term colchicine therapy.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider asking about B12 testing if colchicine is part of your long-term plan.

depletions.mechanism: Cholestyramine binds bile acids in the gut and can interfere with fat digestion and absorption. Because Vitamin A is fat-soluble, long-term bile-acid binding can reduce absorption unless vitamin timing is managed.
depletions.clinical_impact: Lower Vitamin A absorption can matter during long-term cholestyramine use, especially when diet is marginal, fat absorption is already impaired, or pregnancy/nutrition needs are higher.
depletions.recommendation: Review fat-soluble vitamin intake and timing with the prescriber. Vitamins are often separated from cholestyramine so binding in the gut is less likely.
depletions.monitoring_note: Consider Vitamin A monitoring during long-term or high-dose cholestyramine therapy.

sources.label: DailyMed — CHOLESTYRAMINE prescribing information

depletions.alert_headline: May lower vitamin A absorption
depletions.alert_body: Cholestyramine can gradually reduce absorption of Vitamin A over months because this vitamin depends on fat and bile for uptake. Timing vitamins apart can help.
depletions.acknowledgement_note: Good — vitamin A intake helps cover the fat-soluble vitamin gap during cholestyramine use.
depletions.monitoring_tip_short: Consider taking vitamins several hours apart from cholestyramine; confirm timing with your clinician.
